channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric Sign Quantum Computing MOU
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Quantinuum and Mitsubishi Electric represents a significant step toward bringing quantum computing into real-world industrial applications, particularly in manufacturing and electrical systems where Mitsubishi Electric has decades of expertise. The collaboration could accelerate the development of quantum solutions for complex optimization problems that plague modern manufacturing, from supply chain logistics to energy management systems. What makes this particularly noteworthy is Mitsubishi Electric's practical engineering focus, which could help bridge the gap between quantum computing's theoretical promise and actual deployment in industrial settings. This type of industry-academia partnership is becoming increasingly crucial as quantum computing moves from research labs toward commercial viability.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantinuum, a leading quantum computing company, announced today that it has signed a non-binding Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Mitsubishi Electric Corporation, a recognized global leader in the manufacturing, marketing, and sale of electrical and electronic equipment and systems, to establish a framework for a strategic partnership aiming at accelerating […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD Commence Research Collaboration to Develop New Quantum-AI Platform in London
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a significant step toward practical quantum-AI integration, with JPMorganChase becoming one of the first major financial institutions to test quantum algorithms in a dedicated enterprise-grade environment rather than relying solely on cloud access. The partnership combines OQC's quantum hardware expertise with AMD's classical computing infrastructure, creating a hybrid platform that could accelerate the development of quantum advantage applications in financial modeling and risk analysis. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the focus on building secure, on-premises quantum infrastructure in London, potentially establishing a template for how other enterprises might deploy quantum computing capabilities within their own data centers rather than depending entirely on external quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — OQC, JPMorganChase and AMD today announced a research collaboration leveraging a new and dedicated Quantum-AI Data Centre, built by OQC in London. JPMorganChase researchers will test near-term quantum and hybrid quantum-classical computing applications via a secure enterprise environment to examine how quantum computing, AI and high-performance classical infrastructure can work […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: UK Risks Repeating Semiconductor and AI Mistakes Unless Government Acts Now on Quantum, Warns Cross-Party Parliamentary Group
item.description:
Curator's Take: This parliamentary warning highlights a critical inflection point for the UK's quantum ambitions, as the country that pioneered national quantum investment now faces the risk of losing its early-mover advantage without sustained government commitment. The cross-party concern echoes painful lessons from semiconductors and AI, where initial UK leadership gave way to dominance by other nations that maintained stronger, more coordinated public-private partnerships. With quantum technologies approaching commercial viability across computing, sensing, and communications, the timing of this alert couldn't be more crucial - the next few years will likely determine which countries capture the lion's share of what analysts project to be a massive quantum economy. The UK's decade-long head start and £1 billion investment could prove meaningless if competitors like the US, China, and EU continue ramping up their quantum spending while British support wavers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The United Kingdom launched the world’s first National Quantum Technologies Programme in 2014, and has invested more than £1 billion over the past decade in building an ecosystem that spans quantum computing, sensing, communications and timing. According to analysis cited in the paper, quantum computing alone is projected to create […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Download: AI-generated lawsuits and virtual power plants for data centers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article appears to be a general technology newsletter digest rather than focused quantum computing content, covering AI-generated lawsuits and data center power infrastructure. While these topics intersect with the broader tech ecosystem that quantum computing operates within, this particular piece doesn't contain quantum-specific developments or insights that would be directly relevant to quantum computing audiences. The mention of data center power infrastructure could tangentially relate to quantum computing's energy requirements, but without quantum-specific context, this article falls outside the core focus areas that quantum computing enthusiasts would find most valuable.

This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. How courts are coping with a flood of AI-generated lawsuits Most days in her chambers, Judge Maritza Braswell, a federal magistrate judge in Colorado, sifts through stacks of documents written by…

Read original at MIT Technology Review
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum X Labs Adds Oren Raz to Scientific Advisory Board
item.description:
Curator's Take: This appointment signals Quantum X Labs' strategic push to strengthen its scientific foundations by bringing aboard one of Israel's leading theoretical physicists, particularly notable given the Weizmann Institute's stellar reputation in quantum research. Prof. Raz's expertise in statistical mechanics and non-equilibrium physics could prove valuable as the company navigates the complex theoretical challenges inherent in scaling quantum technologies. While advisory board additions are common in the quantum space, securing talent from Israel's premier research institutions suggests Quantum X Labs is serious about bridging the gap between cutting-edge academic research and commercial quantum applications. The timing appears strategic as the company likely prepares for its next phase of development in an increasingly competitive quantum market.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum X Labs Inc. (Nasdaq: QXL), an advanced quantum technologies company, today announces the appointment of Prof. Oren Raz of the Weizmann Institute of Science to its Scientific Advisory Board. Prof. Raz’s appointment brings one of Israel’s most distinguished physicists into the company’s scientific leadership at a pivotal moment in […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: C12 Introduces Nanoassembly Process for Carbon Nanotube Quantum Chips
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a significant manufacturing breakthrough that could reshape how quantum processors are built, as C12's Pick & Place nanoassembly technology addresses one of the most challenging aspects of carbon nanotube quantum computing: precisely positioning individual nanotubes on silicon chips. Carbon nanotube qubits offer compelling advantages including long coherence times and compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing, but the field has been held back by the difficulty of reliably placing these nanoscale structures exactly where they need to be. C12's proprietary approach could potentially unlock scalable manufacturing of carbon nanotube quantum processors, offering an alternative pathway to the silicon and superconducting qubit platforms that currently dominate the industry. This development represents the kind of foundational engineering advance that often precedes major leaps in quantum computing capability and commercialization.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — C12, a Paris-based quantum computing company developing carbon nanotube spin qubit processors, today announced its proprietary Pick & Place nanoassembly technology: a custom-built, patented nanoassembly method developed entirely in-house to transfer individual carbon nanotubes onto silicon chips with micrometric precision, in controlled vacuum or inert atmosphere conditions. Two setups are […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuantX Labs Announces Seed Round With Serendipity Capital Lead Investor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding announcement signals growing investor confidence in quantum sensing applications beyond the traditional focus on quantum computing, with QuantX Labs targeting the critical market of positioning, navigation and timing systems. Optical atomic clocks represent one of the most commercially viable near-term quantum technologies, offering precision timing capabilities essential for everything from GPS systems to financial networks where microsecond accuracy matters. The defense and space focus is particularly noteworthy, as these sectors are driving significant demand for quantum-enhanced navigation systems that can operate independently of GPS satellites, addressing both security concerns and the need for robust positioning in contested environments. Serendipity Capital's investment reflects the broader trend of venture funding shifting toward quantum applications with clearer paths to market deployment.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Serendipity Capital, a leading global investor in critical technologies, announced today that it has led the seed fundraising round for QuantX Labs, an Australian company at the forefront of quantum sensing for applications including Positioning, Navigation and Timing. QuantX Labs is developing optical atomic clock technology for defence, space and […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum to Debut on Nasdaq After Raising $1.68 Billion in IPO
item.description:
Curator's Take: This IPO represents a watershed moment for the quantum computing industry, as Quantinuum becomes one of the first major pure-play quantum companies to go public with such substantial investor backing. The $1.68 billion raise at $60 per share signals growing institutional confidence in quantum computing's commercial viability, particularly given Quantinuum's unique position as the merger of Honeywell Quantum Solutions and Cambridge Quantum Computing, combining hardware and software expertise. This public debut will likely serve as a benchmark for how investors value quantum companies and could pave the way for other quantum startups to access public markets. The success or struggles of Quantinuum's public performance will be closely watched as a barometer for the entire quantum computing sector's maturation from research curiosity to investable technology.

Insider Brief Quantinuum is set to begin trading on the Nasdaq today (June 4) after raising $1.68 billion in one of the largest quantum computing initial public offerings to date, a closely watched debut that could shape investor sentiment across the emerging sector. The Broomfield, Colorado-based company upped the price of its shares at $60 […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Yaqumo, NKT Photonics and Hamamatsu Photonics Sign Quantum Photonics Agreement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership between Yaqumo, NKT Photonics, and Hamamatsu Photonics addresses a crucial but often overlooked bottleneck in quantum computing development: the supply chain for high-performance optical components. While much attention focuses on qubit count and gate fidelities, neutral atom quantum computers depend heavily on sophisticated laser systems and optical devices for atom trapping, manipulation, and readout. This collaboration signals the quantum industry's maturation beyond pure research, as companies recognize that reliable, industrial-grade optical components are essential for scaling quantum systems from laboratory prototypes to commercial products. The move reflects growing awareness that quantum computing success requires not just better algorithms and processors, but also a robust ecosystem of specialized suppliers and manufacturing partners.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — As the industrialization of quantum computing accelerates worldwide, the advancement and stable procurement of optical core components and materials that fundamentally underpin system performance have become critical challenges — beyond the development of quantum processors alone. In cold-atom (neutral atom) quantum computers in particular, high-performance optical devices are essential at […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Design Acquires Quantum Sensing Company Qnami
item.description:
Curator's Take: This acquisition signals a significant consolidation in the quantum sensing market as established instrumentation giant Quantum Design absorbs diamond NV-center specialist Qnami, combining decades of materials characterization expertise with cutting-edge quantum sensing capabilities. The move reflects growing commercial interest in quantum sensing applications, particularly for semiconductor and materials research where diamond-based magnetometry can detect magnetic fields with unprecedented precision at the nanoscale. This merger could accelerate the deployment of quantum sensing tools in industrial settings, bridging the gap between academic research and practical applications in areas like quality control for quantum devices and advanced materials development. The acquisition also demonstrates how traditional scientific instrument companies are positioning themselves in the quantum technology ecosystem by incorporating quantum sensing into their broader portfolio of research tools.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quantum Design International, Inc., a leading global provider of scientific instrumentation and advanced materials characterization solutions, announced the acquisition of Qnami, a pioneer in diamond-based quantum sensing technologies. The transaction enhances Quantum Design’s ability to support researchers and industrial customers working at the frontiers of quantum materials, spintronics, nanomagnetism, semiconductors, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quobly Closes €115 Million ($133.5 Million USD) Series A to Industrialize Silicon-Spin Qubit Processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding milestone represents a significant vote of confidence in silicon-spin qubit technology, which promises superior manufacturability compared to more exotic quantum computing approaches by leveraging existing semiconductor fabrication infrastructure. Quobly's substantial Series A round, particularly with backing from France's strategic Deep Tech 2030 initiative and cybersecurity firm SEALSQ, signals Europe's commitment to building domestic quantum capabilities that could challenge the current dominance of superconducting and trapped-ion platforms. The involvement of a post-quantum cryptography specialist as a co-lead investor is particularly telling, suggesting the funding is positioned not just for quantum computing development but for the broader quantum-safe transition that organizations worldwide are beginning to navigate. With silicon-spin qubits potentially offering easier scaling pathways due to their compatibility with conventional chip manufacturing, Quobly's industrialization push could accelerate the timeline for practical quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Grenoble-based hardware venture Quobly has finalized a €115 million ($133.5 million USD) Series A financing round to transition its silicon-based quantum computing architecture from technology validation into active industrial execution. The capital expansion was co-led by Bpifrance (investing via the Deep Tech 2030 fund under the France 2030 initiative), post-quantum cybersecurity specialist SEALSQ, and semiconductor [...] The post Quobly Closes €115 Million ($133.5 Million USD) Series A to Industrialize Silicon-Spin Qubit Processors appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Design Completes Acquisition of Qnami to Expand Nitrogen-Vacancy Diamond Sensing Portfolio
item.description:
Curator's Take: This acquisition signals the growing maturation and commercialization of quantum sensing technologies, particularly nitrogen-vacancy (NV) diamond sensors that can detect magnetic fields with extraordinary precision. Quantum Design's strategic consolidation of specialized quantum sensing companies reflects the increasing market demand for these instruments across applications ranging from medical imaging to materials science research. The deal highlights how established scientific instrumentation companies are positioning themselves to capitalize on the transition of quantum technologies from laboratory curiosities to practical tools, with NV diamond sensors being among the most commercially ready quantum technologies today. This trend of industry consolidation suggests the quantum sensing market is reaching a critical mass where larger players see clear value in acquiring specialized quantum hardware capabilities.

Quantum Design International, Inc. has finalized the acquisition of Basel-based hardware developer Qnami, marking its second corporate consolidation action of 2026. The transaction integrates Qnami's proprietary diamond-based quantum sensing assets into Quantum Design's global scientific instrumentation portfolio. The acquisition follows Quantum Design's January 2026 procurement of the NanoScience division from Oxford Instruments, positioning the parent [...] The post Quantum Design Completes Acquisition of Qnami to Expand Nitrogen-Vacancy Diamond Sensing Portfolio appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ooredoo Implements Quantum Key Distribution Link on Qatar’s Core Dark Fiber Infrastructure
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant milestone as Qatar becomes one of the first nations to integrate quantum key distribution directly into its national telecommunications infrastructure, moving QKD beyond laboratory demonstrations into real-world operational use. The collaboration between Ooredoo, academia, and the Ministry of Defense reflects the growing recognition among governments that quantum-safe communications are becoming a national security imperative as quantum computers threaten current encryption standards. What's particularly noteworthy is the integration into existing dark fiber infrastructure, demonstrating how QKD can be practically deployed using current telecom assets rather than requiring entirely new networks. This implementation could serve as a blueprint for other nations looking to quantum-secure their critical communications infrastructure before large-scale quantum computers emerge.

Ooredoo Qatar, in a joint national initiative with Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and the Ministry of Defense, has completed the deployment of Qatar’s first quantum-safe communications link. This infrastructure project establishes a fully operational, end-to-end Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) framework integrated directly into a live telecommunications environment. Developed to address the strategic risk of [...] The post Ooredoo Implements Quantum Key Distribution Link on Qatar’s Core Dark Fiber Infrastructure appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft, Atom Computing, EeroQ update their quantum computing progress
item.description:
Curator's Take: This brief update from Ars Technica highlights how major quantum computing players are maintaining steady momentum with regular progress reports, which signals the industry's growing maturity and commercial viability. While the summary doesn't detail specific breakthroughs, the fact that Microsoft, Atom Computing, and EeroQ are all simultaneously sharing updates suggests coordinated efforts to demonstrate real advancement rather than just research milestones. These regular progress reports are becoming increasingly important as quantum computing transitions from purely academic pursuit to a competitive commercial landscape where companies must prove tangible development to investors and potential enterprise customers. The convergence of updates from both tech giants like Microsoft and specialized quantum startups indicates the field is entering a critical phase where consistent progress reporting becomes as important as the underlying technology itself.

Some quantum computing companies we've covered have done recent progress updates.

Read original at Ars Technica - Science
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nanomagnets control diamond qubits, pointing to more scalable quantum hardware
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research addresses one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: creating scalable hardware architectures that can maintain qubit quality while growing to practical sizes. Diamond-based qubits, particularly nitrogen-vacancy centers, are already known for their exceptional coherence properties, and the addition of nanomagnetic control represents a potentially game-changing approach to precise qubit manipulation without the bulky infrastructure typically required. The Virginia Commonwealth University team's work could pave the way for more compact, room-temperature quantum processors that are easier to manufacture and deploy outside specialized laboratory environments. While still in early stages, this type of hardware innovation is exactly what's needed to bridge the gap between today's proof-of-concept quantum computers and the large-scale systems that could revolutionize computing across industries.

Quantum computing, once only a theoretical possibility, promises to deliver faster, more energy-efficient computers—but only if scientists can build and scale the hardware needed to run the machines. New research from Virginia Commonwealth University brings scientists one small step closer to quantum computing at a practical scale, which could help dramatically reduce energy usage and computing times in some industries.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Ooredoo, HBKU, Ministry of Defence Launch Qatar’s First Quantum-Safe Network
item.description:
Curator's Take: This deployment marks a significant milestone as Qatar becomes one of the first nations in the Middle East to establish quantum-safe communications infrastructure, positioning itself ahead of the curve for post-quantum cybersecurity. The collaboration between a major telecom provider, academic institution, and defense ministry demonstrates how quantum-resistant technologies are transitioning from research labs to real-world national security applications. This initiative reflects the growing urgency among nations to upgrade their communications infrastructure before large-scale quantum computers potentially render current encryption methods obsolete. Qatar's proactive approach could serve as a model for other countries looking to build quantum-resilient digital infrastructure while there's still time to implement these protections methodically.

Insider Brief Ooredoo Qatar announced the deployment of the first quantum-safe communications link in Qatar, a project the company says establishes a foundation for next-generation cybersecurity and secure digital infrastructure in the country. According to the Qatar Tribune, the initiative was developed in partnership with Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and Qatar’s Ministry of Defence. […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Atom Computing Reaches Quantum Error Correction Milestone with Toric Code Demonstration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This demonstration by Atom Computing represents a crucial step toward practical quantum computing, as the company successfully showed that adding more physical qubits actually reduces logical error rates using toric code error correction. This achievement is particularly significant because it validates the theoretical promise of quantum error correction in a real hardware system, moving beyond small-scale proof-of-concept experiments to meaningful error suppression. Atom Computing's neutral-atom platform continues to demonstrate competitive advantages in scaling and connectivity, making this milestone especially noteworthy as the field races toward fault-tolerant quantum computers. The successful implementation of toric codes, which are among the most promising error correction schemes for two-dimensional qubit layouts, could accelerate the timeline for achieving practical quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Logical error rate vs. cycles of error correction in our quantum memory experiment Atom Computing has completed a demonstration of quantum error correction using a toric code configuration on its neutral-atom quantum computing system. The validation metrics indicate that the platform reduces logical error rates when scaling up physical qubit allocations, a characteristic known as [...] The post Atom Computing Reaches Quantum Error Correction Milestone with Toric Code Demonstration appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High-fidelity neutral atom gates leveraging low-rank Hessian optimization
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a breakthrough in making quantum optimal control practically viable by solving one of its biggest experimental hurdles: efficiently calibrating the complex, high-dimensional waveforms needed for high-fidelity gates. The researchers cleverly exploit the mathematical structure of quantum control landscapes to identify just the few critical directions that actually matter for gate performance, dramatically reducing calibration time from what would otherwise be an intractable search through thousands of parameters. Their demonstration of a 99.9% fidelity controlled-Z gate on neutral atom qubits that remains robust under significant laser power variations showcases both the method's effectiveness and its practical resilience. This low-rank Hessian approach could become a standard tool across quantum computing platforms, finally making sophisticated optimal control gates accessible for real quantum algorithms rather than just theoretical demonstrations.

Quantum optimal control can produce fast and robust multi-qubit gates, but experimentally calibrating the resulting high-dimensional waveforms remains challenging because direct searches over large parameter spaces converge slowly. Building on the low-rank structure of quantum-control landscapes, we develop and benchmark a Hessian-based calibration method for optimal-control gates. The method identifies the few waveform directions that affect fidelity to leading order, with the number of directions set by the accessible leakage and coherent error channels, and optimizes only within this principal space using closed-loop experimental feedback. We apply this approach to an amplitude-robust controlled-Z gate on metastable-state 171Yb nuclear-spin qubits. Experimentally, we verify the predicted Hessian-sensitive directions and demonstrate rapid convergence of the optimization protocol. The optimized gate reaches a raw fidelity of 0.9959(2), increasing to 0.99902(7) after postselection on no detected loss, and the performance is essentially unchanged under laser-power variations of up to 20%. We further show that the same fidelity Hessian directions can correct certain Hamiltonian parameter errors. These results establish low-rank Hessian optimization as an efficient and physically motivated calibration strategy for high-dimensional optimal-control gates, which is broadly applicable to many qubit types.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Measuring Entanglement in Qubit System
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a clever experimental framework that connects three fundamental quantum phenomena - coherence loss, quantum erasure, and entanglement - under a unified theoretical umbrella using interferometry setups. The key breakthrough is demonstrating how entanglement between internal degrees of freedom and ancillary systems can be directly measured through observable asymmetries in interferometer outputs, even when the ancilla itself cannot be directly accessed experimentally. This work provides quantum researchers with a practical toolbox for quantifying entanglement in real qubit systems using standard interferometric techniques, potentially making entanglement characterization more accessible across different experimental platforms from spin systems to photonic circuits. The unified perspective on these seemingly separate quantum effects could lead to new insights for quantum sensing applications and more robust methods for validating quantum device performance.

An operational way of measuring entanglement in a balanced two-path interferometers is presented, where path information is carried by some internal degree of freedom which, in turn, gets entangled with an ancilla system. The analysis is based on a tripartite description involving paths, an internal qubit degree of freedom, and some ancillary states entangled with the internal degree of freedom. It is then applied to two physically distinct experimental situations: a modified Stern-Gerlach interferometer with spin-1/2 particles and a Mach-Zehnder interferometer with photons carrying polarization. The ancilla degree of freedom may not be experimentally accessible. Tracing out the ancillary system, and employing a quantum erasing procedure based on the internal degree of freedom, it is demonstrated that a concurrence-based measure of the entanglement, between the internal degree of freedom and the ancilla, can be extracted directly from measurable asymmetry of the two output channels. These results show that loss of coherence, quantum erasure, and entanglement estimation in interferometric experiments arise from the same underlying correlation structure and provide a compact experimentally accessible framework for quantifying entanglement in qubit systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Atom Computing Reveals Quantum Error Correction with Toric Code
item.description:
Curator's Take: This achievement marks a significant milestone for Atom Computing's neutral atom platform, as the toric code is considered one of the most promising quantum error correction schemes for building fault-tolerant quantum computers. The demonstration that error rates actually decrease as more qubits are added to the computation represents a critical threshold in quantum computing development, showing that the system has crossed the break-even point where additional qubits help rather than hurt computational fidelity. This places Atom Computing alongside Google and IBM as companies that have achieved sustained quantum error correction over multiple rounds, though the neutral atom approach offers unique advantages in qubit connectivity and scalability. The toric code implementation is particularly noteworthy because it provides a pathway toward topological quantum computing, which could offer inherently more stable quantum operations for future large-scale quantum computers.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Atom Computing today announced the industry’s first full demonstration of quantum error correction using a toric code. The results show that the company’s neutral-atom system reduces errors as larger numbers of qubits are used in computations, placing Atom Computing among only two companies that have demonstrated many rounds of sustained […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Measurement-induced state transitions in multi-qubit transmon processors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical but often overlooked challenge in superconducting quantum processors: how measuring one qubit can inadvertently disturb neighboring qubits through measurement-induced state transitions (MIST). While previous work focused on isolated qubits, this study reveals that the crosstalk between qubits in real multi-qubit chips can actually lower the threshold for these unwanted transitions, potentially corrupting quantum information during readout operations. The findings provide essential insights for designing better quantum processors, as understanding and mitigating measurement crosstalk becomes increasingly important as quantum computers scale to larger numbers of qubits. This work offers practical guidance for optimizing readout protocols in current superconducting quantum devices while highlighting fundamental physics that could impact the fidelity of quantum computations.

Dispersive readout of the transmon qubit in circuit QED is known to lose its quantum non-demolition character at small to moderate measurement drive amplitudes. This phenomenon is understood to originate from Laundau-Zener transitions at accidental multi-photon resonances, where $n$ drive photons can promote the transmon by $m$ levels. This interpretation has been shown to be in agreement with experiments characterizing the dispersive readout of a single transmon. The impact of these measurement-induced state transition (MIST) of a transmon embedded in a multi-qubit chip, however, remains largely unexplored. Here, we show that the presence of other components, such as qubits and couplers, can affect the MIST threshold of a measured transmon. To arrive at these results, we present a general method to characterize measurement-induced transition when the qubit under readout is coupled to other circuit elements, a ubiquitous situation in circuit QED-based quantum processors. As an example, we consider the case of two transmon qubits, and we show that the spectator qubit can be impacted by the measurement-induced transition of the readout qubit and, conversely, that the presence of the spectator qubit can lower the MIST threshold of the readout qubit. Finally, we explore how adding a coupler mode between the two qubits further modifies these effects.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An Entanglement Measure from Quantum Optimal Transport
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a groundbreaking approach to measuring quantum entanglement by borrowing concepts from optimal transport theory, a mathematical framework traditionally used in economics and logistics. The proposed measure elegantly satisfies all the fundamental requirements for quantifying entanglement within a single geometric framework, potentially offering a more unified and computationally tractable way to characterize this essential quantum resource. What makes this particularly exciting is the direct connection to experimental entanglement detection through entanglement witnesses, providing a bridge between abstract theoretical measures and practical quantum state verification. This work could lead to more efficient algorithms for entanglement quantification and better methods for validating entangled states in quantum devices, making it valuable for both fundamental research and near-term quantum applications.

We propose a bipartite entanglement measure $E(ρ)$ defined as the minimal order-1 quantum Wasserstein distance from a state to the set of separable states. Owing to the universal data-processing inequality of the Wasserstein metric, the measure satisfies all fundamental axioms within a single geometric framework. A Lipschitz dual formulation yields explicit lower bounds for pure and mixed states, a sharp constant for two-qubit systems, and an expected value for Haar-random pure states. We further establish a quantitative connection to entanglement witnesses: any negative witness expectation value certifies a lower bound on $E$, and the dual variational bound is exactly the maximal violation achievable by a Lipschitz-1 witness. The approach naturally provides subadditivity, trace-distance estimates, and bounds on local observables, while pointing toward large-deviation conjectures. This work furnishes a versatile paradigm at the interface of entanglement theory, optimal transport, and experimental entanglement detection.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Expressibility, Noise, and Error Mitigation in VQE Ansatz Selection
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a critical practical challenge in quantum computing: how to choose the best quantum circuits for solving chemistry problems when real quantum hardware is noisy and imperfect. The findings reveal that expressibility, a popular theoretical metric for ranking quantum circuits, becomes nearly useless under realistic noise conditions, and surprisingly, common error mitigation techniques like probabilistic error cancellation often make performance worse rather than better. Most encouragingly, the study shows that simple, easily-calculated properties like the number of two-qubit gates in a circuit can actually predict performance just as well as complex theoretical metrics, offering quantum algorithm designers a much more practical tool for circuit selection. This work provides valuable guidance for researchers developing near-term quantum algorithms, suggesting they may be overthinking circuit design and should focus on simpler, more robust approaches.

The variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) is a promising algorithm for near-term quantum chemistry applications, but selecting optimal ansatz circuits remains challenging. Expressibility, a metric quantifying a circuit's ability to explore the Hilbert space, has been proposed as a guide for ansatz selection, but recent work showed it inconsistently predicts VQE performance under realistic noise for $H_2$. We extend this investigation to cover both $H_2$ and $H_3^+$ under four execution scenarios: ideal, noisy, and noisy with zero-noise extrapolation (ZNE) or probabilistic error cancellation (PEC). We find that error mitigation does not reliably restore expressibility's predictive power. ZNE reduces error for only 4 of 12 $H_2$ circuits and 4 of 6 $H_3^+$ circuits, while PEC actually increases error in 11 of 12 $H_2$ circuits and all 6 $H_3^+$ circuits. We reproduce and extend Saib et al.'s key finding that circuit rankings scramble under noise (Spearman $ρ\approx -0.1$ between ideal and noisy rankings), and identify a new result: ZNE largely preserves noisy rankings ($ρ= +0.80$ for $H_2$) while PEC actively reorders them ($ρ= -0.22$). Noisy expressibility, computed from density matrix simulations, strongly predicts unmitigated performance for $H_3^+$ (Pearson $r = +0.91$, $p = 0.01$), but this metric is computationally intractable at scale. We demonstrate that zero-cost circuit topology metrics such as two-qubit gate count provide comparable or superior predictive power for PEC degradation ($r = +0.96$ for $H_3^+$), while standard expressibility best predicts noisy and ZNE performance for $H_2$ ($r = +0.74$ and $r = +0.77$).

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Phase-correlation-free quantum key distribution source operating at gigahertz rates
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical security vulnerability in high-speed quantum key distribution systems, where traditional laser sources can develop phase correlations at gigahertz rates that could potentially be exploited by eavesdroppers. The researchers' innovative use of a super-luminescent LED (SLED) operating at 1.25 GHz offers an elegant solution that provides intrinsic phase randomization through its spontaneous emission nature, eliminating the need for complex active randomization schemes. What makes this particularly exciting is that SLED technology is both compact and cost-effective compared to traditional approaches, potentially making secure quantum communications more accessible while maintaining the high speeds needed for practical deployment. This work represents an important step toward making quantum cryptography both more secure and more economically viable for real-world applications.

Phase randomization is essential for the security of practical decoy-state quantum key distribution (QKD) systems. Commonly, implementations rely on laser sources which are either actively phase-randomized, or gain-switched. However, at high repetition rates these show correlations, which can ultimately compromise security and performance. We present a 1.25 GHz phase-randomized QKD source based on a super-luminescent light emitting diode (SLED) operating in the C-band as a compact and cost-effective alternative. The source generates $\sim100$ ps optical pulses with $400$ ps pulse-to-pulse separation, compatible with high-speed time-bin encoding. Interferometric measurements demonstrate $>99\%$ visibility between adjacent time bins, confirming strong first-order coherence within the same quantum signals, while the spontaneous-emission-driven nature of the SLED ensures intrinsic global phase randomization between adjacent signals. This work establishes a scalable SLED-based platform for high-speed prepare-and-measure QKD systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: AI-Powered Quantum: Microsoft Turns to Agentic AI to Speed Quantum Computing Push
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating reversal in the quantum-AI relationship: while much attention focuses on how quantum computers might eventually accelerate AI, Microsoft is demonstrating how today's AI can immediately accelerate quantum development itself. By deploying agentic AI systems to tackle complex quantum computing challenges, Microsoft is essentially using current AI capabilities as a bridge to reach quantum computing milestones faster. This approach could significantly compress the timeline for quantum breakthroughs by automating much of the tedious optimization and problem-solving work that currently requires extensive human expertise. The strategy represents a pragmatic near-term application that could give Microsoft a competitive edge in the quantum race while we wait for quantum computers to mature enough to revolutionize AI in return.

Insider Brief As quantum AI — the idea that quantum computers could one day enhance artificial intelligence — steadily climbs the list of anticipated quantum computing use cases, Microsoft says AI is already playing a growing role in advancing its quantum computing program. The company said it is increasingly relying on agentic artificial intelligence to […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Pushing the Classical Frontier of 1D Fermi-Hubbard Quench Dynamics Beyond Current Quantum Simulations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work delivers a significant reality check to recent quantum advantage claims by dramatically improving the classical simulation baseline that quantum computers are measured against. The researchers achieved bond dimensions 15 times larger than previous benchmarks using GPU acceleration and clever exploitation of symmetries, allowing them to fully verify the quantum simulation results that were previously unconfirmed in the most challenging high-entanglement regime. Most notably, they pushed classical simulation beyond what the quantum hardware achieved, reaching evolution time t=7 compared to the quantum experiment's t=6 limit. This highlights the ongoing cat-and-mouse game between quantum and classical computing, where apparent quantum advantages can evaporate as classical algorithms and hardware continue to improve at a rapid pace.

Establishing quantum advantage requires comparison against the best achievable classical simulation. The Q-CTRL team recently simulated quench dynamics of the one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model on an IBM processor, completing a $L=60$ evolution to time $t=6$ in under three minutes and claiming a $3000\times$ speedup over classical Time-Dependent Variational Principle (TDVP) simulation at bond dimension $χ=4096$. Their classical benchmark required over 160 hours on a CPU cluster, failed to converge in the high-entanglement regime $t\in[5.2,6]$, and left the most challenging window of the experiment unverified. Here, we push the boundaries of classical simulation by exploiting the full $\mathrm{U}(1)\times\mathrm{SU}(2)$ symmetry of the Fermi-Hubbard Hamiltonian combined with GPU-accelerated tensor contractions. Reaching bond dimensions up to $χ\approx62{,}000$ on four NVIDIA H200 GPUs -- among the largest ever achieved in TDVP simulations and fifteen times larger than Q-CTRL's classical baseline -- we achieve fully converged results across the entire simulation window, including rigorous certification of the previously unresolved high-entanglement regime $t\in[5.2,6]$. We further advance the classical frontier to $t=7$, which lies beyond the quantum hardware experiment and any previously verified classical evolution of the full wavefunction. At the bond dimension comparable to Q-CTRL's best classical run, our GPU implementation completes in $\sim\!100$ minutes, directly reducing the claimed $3000\times$ quantum advantage to $\sim\!36\times$. These results substantially narrow the quantum-classical performance gap and establish a new standard for tensor-network benchmarking of large-scale quantum simulations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Imaginarity witness
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article advances the theoretical foundation of quantum resource theory by developing new mathematical tools to detect and measure "imaginarity" - a quantum property related to complex numbers that serves as a resource for quantum information processing. The researchers establish that any nonreal Hermitian operator can act as an "imaginarity witness," creating a systematic framework similar to the well-established entanglement witnesses that help identify quantum entanglement in experimental systems. What makes this particularly significant is their proof that their new "witnessed imaginarity" measure aligns perfectly with existing measures like trace norm and robustness of imaginarity, providing multiple equivalent ways to quantify this quantum resource. This theoretical work could lead to more efficient methods for characterizing and utilizing imaginarity in quantum algorithms and protocols, adding another tool to the quantum information theorist's toolkit.

Imaginarity has shown to be an important resource in quantum information. The witness theory of quantum resource, such as entanglement witness, coherence witness, and imaginarity witness, has been established, in particular entanglement witness and coherence witness have been extensively explored. We present here another class of imaginarity witnesses beyond the one in [{Phys. Lett. A} \textbf{530}, 130135 (2025)]. Within our framework, any nonreal Hermitian operator under the reference basis is an imaginarity witness and only finite of them can detect all the imaginarity states. As the common approaches that were explored in the witness theories of entanglement and coherence, we then explore the relations between these imaginarity witnesses in different cases: (i) when they can detect common imaginary states, (ii) when they can detect the same sets of imaginary states, and (ii) when they obey the finer relation. Finally, we define an imaginarity measure in terms of witnesses, termed witnessed imaginarity, and prove that it coincides with both the trace norm of imaginarity and the robustness of imaginarity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum influencers gather to celebrate London’s role in quantum tech
item.description:
Curator's Take: This gathering of quantum leaders in London highlights the UK capital's emerging role as a major global hub for quantum technology development, bringing together key figures from academia, industry, and government to discuss the city's quantum ecosystem. The formation of organized quantum clusters like London's represents a crucial step in translating quantum research breakthroughs into commercial applications, as these networks facilitate collaboration between startups, established tech companies, and research institutions. London's positioning as a quantum center is particularly significant given the UK government's substantial investments in quantum technologies and the city's existing strengths in fintech and deep tech innovation, suggesting we may see accelerated development of practical quantum applications in finance and other sectors.

Matin Durrani reports from a meeting of the London Quantum Cluster The post Quantum influencers gather to celebrate London’s role in quantum tech appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantification of Entanglement in Three-Qubit Systems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: understanding and measuring entanglement in multi-qubit systems, which becomes exponentially more complex as you add qubits beyond simple two-qubit pairs. The researchers introduce a new mathematical framework for quantifying three-qubit entanglement that could provide clearer insights into how quantum correlations distribute across small quantum circuits, which is crucial for optimizing quantum algorithms and error correction schemes. While this work is highly theoretical, better entanglement measures are essential for quantum computer designers who need to understand exactly how their qubits interact and maintain coherence during computation. The fact that their measure provides "complementary insights" to existing tools suggests it could become part of a more complete toolkit for characterizing the quantum states that power next-generation quantum processors.

This study presents an analytical investigation of entanglement quantification in three-qubit pure states through the Acín canonical representation, which serves as a generalization of the two-qubit Schmidt decomposition. Driven by the intricacies of multipartite entanglement and the shortcomings of current measures, we employ a global concurrence measure derived from the generalized concurrence of various bipartitions within the system. The characteristics of this measure are explored both analytically and numerically across a range of SLOCC entanglement classes, such as product, biseparable, GHZ-type, and W-type states. When compared to the three-tangle and tripartite negativity, our findings indicate that this measure yields complementary insights into multipartite correlations. The outcomes underscore the significance of this measure in elucidating the structure and distribution of entanglement within three-qubit quantum systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QPredSGG: Hybrid Quantum Predicate Learning for Long-Tailed Scene Graph Generation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating application of quantum computing to computer vision, specifically addressing the notorious "long-tail" problem in scene graph generation where AI models struggle to recognize rare object relationships due to dataset imbalances. The researchers achieved a remarkable 256× parameter reduction while actually improving performance on recognizing infrequent predicates, suggesting quantum circuits may have unique advantages for handling imbalanced classification tasks that plague many real-world AI applications. What makes this particularly significant is the practical demonstration on a standard computer vision benchmark (Visual Genome 150) rather than a toy problem, showing quantum machine learning moving closer to solving actual industry challenges. The dramatic parameter efficiency could be transformative for deploying AI models on resource-constrained devices, though the work will need validation on larger datasets and real quantum hardware to prove its broader applicability.

Scene Graph Generation (SGG) requires relational reasoning over objects and their interactions, but performance is often limited by severe long-tail predicate imbalance. Classical SGG models frequently rely on dataset statistics, leading to biased predictions toward frequent relations rather than fine-grained semantic predicates. Although existing debiasing strategies improve mean recall, predicate classification in current frameworks still often depends on large classical decision modules with high parameter cost. This work introduces a hybrid quantum predicate classifier for SGG by replacing the classical predicate head in Causal Feature Enhancement Network (CFEN) with a Quantum Predicate Head (QP-Head) trained using weighted cross-entropy. To the best of our knowledge, this is among the first studies to evaluate a hybrid quantum architecture for scene graph predicate classification on Visual Genome 150. We study the effect of qubit count, encoding strategy, entangling structure, and circuit depth on relational prediction. The best 4-qubit QP-Head uses Amplitude Embedding and Strongly Entangling Layers to compress 4096-dimensional pair features into a 16-dimensional quantum-compatible representation, corresponding to a 256$\times$ reduction. It achieves an mR@100 of 57.25%, compared with 41.1% for the classical CFEN reference, while using only 96 trainable quantum parameters. Scaling to 8 qubits maintains strong long-tail performance, reaching an mR@100 of 55.38% with 384 quantum parameters, while the depth analysis shows a trade-off between expressibility and runtime overhead. These results suggest that compact hybrid quantum predicate heads can support parameter-efficient long-tail relational classification in complex visual reasoning tasks.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Digital Quantum Reservoir Computing for ATM Time Series Prediction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents an important step toward practical quantum machine learning applications by testing quantum reservoir computing on real financial data with actual quantum hardware, moving beyond toy problems to tackle genuine forecasting challenges. The researchers' use of ATM cash demand prediction provides a concrete benchmark that demonstrates both the current limitations and emerging potential of near-term quantum devices for time series analysis. While the quantum models didn't outperform classical methods in standard metrics, their superior performance in capturing temporal patterns suggests quantum reservoir computing may offer unique advantages for understanding sequential data structures that classical approaches miss. The systematic comparison across noiseless simulation, noise-aware emulation, and real quantum hardware provides valuable insights into how quantum noise affects practical machine learning performance.

We investigate a digital quantum reservoir computing (QRC) framework for multi-step forecasting of automated teller machine (ATM) cash demand time series on near-term quantum devices. The proposed approach uses parametrized four-qubit reservoirs with a fixed structure exploiting partial measurement and reset, where temporal data is encoded in rotation angles. Training is restricted to a classical Ridge-regression readout. We systematically analyze the impact of the circuit ansatzë, reservoir memory, measurement-derived observables, and the execution backend on the forecasting performance. Experiments are performed with noiseless simulation, noise-aware emulation, and a real IQM Spark quantum processor. Although the QRC models do not outperform the classical Prophet benchmark in terms of Mean Absolute Error and Normalized Mean Squared Error metrics, they achieve more competitive results in Dynamic Time Warping metric, indicating a partial ability to capture temporal structure. These findings provide an empirical assessment of digital QRC for realistic financial forecasting and highlight both its current limitations and its potential on near-term quantum hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nonreciprocal Dispersive Coupling for Quantum Sensing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever twist on dispersive coupling that could significantly enhance quantum sensor precision by breaking the usual symmetry constraints. The researchers demonstrate that nonreciprocal dispersive coupling outperforms traditional reciprocal methods when measuring cavity photon numbers, with the advantage becoming more pronounced at higher photon counts - a finding that could lead to more sensitive quantum magnetometers, accelerometers, and other precision instruments. Particularly intriguing is their strategy of converting driving strength measurements into photon number measurements to leverage this nonreciprocal advantage, suggesting a general framework for optimizing quantum sensing protocols. While still in the theoretical realm, this work points toward a new design principle for quantum sensors that could push measurement precision closer to fundamental quantum limits.

Dispersive coupling is widely utilized for quantum information readout. Most prior studies have concentrated on reciprocal dispersive coupling. Here, we further construct nonreciprocal dispersive coupling and apply it to quantum sensing. For cavity photon number measurement, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling delivers higher precision than its reciprocal counterpart, and this advantage grows more pronounced with an increase in photon number. When directly measuring the single-photon driving strength, however, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling shows no superiority over reciprocal dispersive coupling. By converting the information of driving strength into cavity photon numbers via our proposed strategy, nonreciprocal dispersive coupling again outperforms reciprocal dispersive coupling in precision, with the advantage becoming increasingly significant at larger driving strength. This work presents a novel method to boost quantum sensing and enable the fabrication of ultra-precise quantum sensors.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Circuit-Level Noise Estimation via Shuttling in Plaquette Circuits
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a critical challenge in scaling quantum error correction: how to estimate noise levels in quantum circuits when you can only take single measurements and don't have full control over the hardware calibration. The researchers cleverly use surface code plaquette experiments with two different qubit management strategies to extract noise information from limited data, which is especially valuable for cloud-based quantum computing where users lack low-level hardware access. By testing their approach on both ion-trap and superconducting systems, they demonstrate a practical method for characterizing error correction performance that could help researchers optimize QEC protocols even with restricted hardware access. This work addresses a real bottleneck in quantum error correction development, where understanding your noise landscape is essential but often requires privileged access to quantum hardware that most users simply don't have.

We present a method for estimating QEC circuit-level noise levels assuming that only single-shot measurements are available (e.g. measurements are slow and performed in a zoned/parallel fashion), and that lower level quantum hardware calibration is not possible (e.g. cloud access) or not feasible (e.g. large scale computing). We develop and run surface code plaquette experiments using two syndrome qubit configurations: FRESH, involving fresh qubits for each plaquette repetition, and RECYCLE, reusing qubits. To validate our approach, we compile plaquettes to ion-trap (IonQ Aria1) native gate set and apply hardware-aware rewrite templates to reduce circuit depth and execution time. We also run the experiments on a non-shuttling, superconducting processor (IBM Torino). We estimate circuit-level noise rates from the resulting single-shot plaquette measurement statistics, and conclude numerically about the viability of low-depth QEC experiments.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Qunova Joins JHPC-Quantum Test User Program in Japan, Providing Algorithms For Advanced Industrial Computation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a significant step in Japan's national quantum strategy, as Qunova's optimization algorithms will now be tested on Japan's ambitious high-performance computing infrastructure through the JHPC-quantum initiative. The collaboration highlights how countries are increasingly prioritizing quantum software development alongside hardware investments, recognizing that practical quantum applications require sophisticated algorithmic frameworks. Qunova's focus on industrial computation problems could accelerate real-world quantum adoption in manufacturing and logistics sectors, areas where Japan has deep expertise and economic interests. This type of government-industry partnership demonstrates the maturing quantum ecosystem, where specialized software companies are becoming integral to national quantum competitiveness strategies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Qunova Computing, a pioneering maker of software for quantum computing, has signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with JHPC-quantum (Japan High-Performance Computing), a landmark Japanese national computing initiative funded by NEDO (New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization) under Japan’s Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry. The agreement designates Qunova […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Report: Quantum Computing Could Unlock More Than £100 Million in Value
item.description:
Curator's Take: This D-Wave commissioned survey reveals surprisingly bullish short-term expectations among UK enterprises, with nearly half anticipating nine-figure quantum value creation within just one year. While such aggressive timelines may reflect more optimism than current quantum capabilities can deliver, the findings underscore how seriously large organizations are now taking quantum computing as a strategic priority rather than a distant research curiosity. The survey's focus on immediate value creation suggests businesses are primarily looking at quantum annealing applications for optimization problems, where D-Wave's systems have demonstrated real-world advantages today, rather than waiting for fault-tolerant quantum computers that remain years away. This shift from "quantum someday" to "quantum now" thinking, even if overly ambitious, signals a maturing market where quantum solutions are increasingly viewed as practical business tools.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Forty-one percent of large UK enterprises surveyed estimate quantum computing could unlock more than £100 million in value to their business in as little as one year, according to new findings from Censuswide and D-Wave Quantum Inc. (“D-Wave” or the “Company”). That expectation is also reflected in action: 65% of the UK […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Installs Real-Time Control Component for Universal Photonic Quantum Computer
item.description:
Curator's Take: This installation of QuiX Quantum's Feed-Forward Control Unit represents a crucial step toward making photonic quantum computers practically viable, as real-time measurement feedback is essential for error correction and complex quantum algorithms in photonic systems. Unlike trapped ion or superconducting approaches, photonic quantum computers face unique challenges in maintaining quantum information flow, making this type of ultra-fast control hardware a critical bottleneck that the field has been working to solve. The FFCU addresses one of the fundamental engineering hurdles that has limited photonic quantum computing's scalability, potentially bringing this promising approach closer to competing with other quantum computing platforms. While still early-stage, this development signals growing maturity in photonic quantum hardware and could accelerate progress toward fault-tolerant photonic quantum systems.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum today announced the first installation of its Feed-Forward Control Unit (FFCU), a high-performance hardware component developed for the company’s universal photonic quantum computing architecture. The FFCU is designed to help the system respond to quantum measurements in real time, an essential requirement for photonic quantum computers that encode and […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Distributed Variational Quantum Optimisation by Entanglement-Selective Transport
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a clever approach to one of quantum computing's most pressing scalability challenges: how to run optimization algorithms across multiple quantum processors without constantly shuttling quantum information between them. QESTO sidesteps the usual bottleneck of expensive non-local gates by using pre-shared Bell pairs as "quantum communication channels" that persist throughout the computation, allowing local operations on each processor to collectively solve global optimization problems. The results showing QESTO outperforming both distributed and monolithic QAOA on tile-matching problems suggest this could be a practical pathway for scaling variational algorithms to larger problem sizes using near-term quantum hardware. This work represents an important step toward distributed quantum computing architectures that could unlock optimization capabilities beyond what single quantum processors can achieve.

Distributed quantum optimisation is challenging because computing the problem cost function across multiple quantum processors requires non-local gates, which can incur overhead in latency and fidelity. Here we introduce QESTO, a distributed variational ansatz for graph-based discrete optimisation that requires only persistent pre-shared Bell pairs for remote operations. Using local operations, it encodes local constraint information in the Bell pairs that is leveraged to produce amplitude transfer towards globally valid distributed solution states. QESTO requires one Bell pair per distributed edge of the problem graph and, after initialisation of the Bell states, uses no non-local gates. On two bounded weighted Wang tile-matching problem ensembles, QESTO achieves stronger convergence to low-cost tilings than equivalently partitioned QAOA with no distributed gates at ansatz depths of two or higher, and exceeds the mean performance of monolithic QAOA at the deepest studied depth in both ensembles. These results suggest that persistent entanglement can support useful variational communication while reducing per-layer non-local gate overhead.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Arbitrary manipulation of nuclear spins in hexagonal boron nitride
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant advancement in developing hexagonal boron nitride as a quantum computing platform, demonstrating how to precisely control nuclear spins using negatively charged boron vacancy centers as intermediaries. The researchers achieved impressive gate fidelities of 99% for single-qubit operations and 95% for two-qubit gates, with ultrafast execution times under 300 nanoseconds that outpace decoherence effects. This work is particularly exciting because it shows how to harness nuclear spins - which typically have much longer coherence times than electron spins - for quantum computation while using the more easily controlled electron spins as auxiliary qubits for manipulation. The combination of high fidelity, speed, and the inherent advantages of hBN as a host material could position this approach as a compelling alternative to other solid-state quantum computing platforms.

Due to its localized nature and controllability, the negatively charged boron vacancy centers (V$_\text{B}^-$) in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) are a promising spin platform for accessing its neighboring nuclei with potential for performing quantum computational tasks. However, the methods of utilizing and manipulating the nuclear spins are still lacking. In this work, we propose a protocol for the preparation of single- and multi-qubit gates on the nuclear spins, utilizing the electron spin as an auxiliary qubit. By applying a background magnetic field and a multi-tone continuous drive, we show that the electron spin coupling to the nuclei can be efficiently engineered. This allows for suppressing the undesired electron-nuclear interactions through the Hahn echo pulse sequence. The target gates are then implemented by employing proper RF drives. Our numerical results for realistic parameters show gate fidelities as high as $99\%$ for single-qubit and $95\%$ for multi-qubit gates. With the gate execution durations being less than $300$ ns, our protocol evades electron spin decoherence effects. Therefore, our scheme sets the stage for the practical application of V$_\text{B}^-$ in hBN for quantum computation purposes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Anisotropic Rabi Model as a Noise Biased Qubit
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work presents an intriguing approach to quantum error correction by exploiting the anisotropic Rabi model's natural bias against certain types of noise, potentially offering a hardware-level solution to one of quantum computing's most persistent challenges. By tuning the anisotropy parameter to selectively suppress dominant decoherence pathways, the researchers demonstrate how the physics of the qubit itself can be engineered to favor certain error modes while suppressing others - a strategy that could complement existing error correction codes. The ability to construct universal gate operations within this protected logical subspace while maintaining robustness against external noise suggests this could be a practical pathway toward more resilient quantum processors. This research represents an exciting convergence of fundamental quantum optics and practical quantum computing, where the underlying physics becomes part of the error correction strategy rather than just the source of errors.

We present the quantum anisotropic Rabi model as a potential resource for a noise biased qubit. The system-environment coupling can be biased by tuning the relative strengths of the rotating-wave and counter-rotating-wave interactions, characterized by the anisotropy parameter $η$. This anisotropy selectively suppresses dominant decoherence pathways, thereby enabling the construction of a protected logical qubit in the ultrastrong and deep-strong coupling regimes. The logical states (formed by the ground and first excited states of the anisotropic Rabi model) possess coherence times that are enhanced compared to the isotropic case. Moreover, we construct a set of universal gate operations within the logical-state subspace and demonstrate that the gate operations associated with different values of $η$ exhibit robustness against external noise. These findings are expected to inspire applications and research directions for the anisotropic Rabi model with promising potential impacts.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quobly Secures €115 Million Series A to Bring Silicon-Based Quantum Computers to Market
item.description:
Curator's Take: This €115 million Series A for Quobly represents one of the largest quantum computing funding rounds in Europe and signals growing investor confidence in silicon-based quantum approaches as a viable alternative to superconducting and trapped-ion systems. The French company's focus on silicon quantum dots is particularly noteworthy because this technology could potentially leverage existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure, offering a path to more scalable and cost-effective quantum computers. Quobly's ambitious 2026 timeline for commercial deployment puts them in direct competition with established players like IBM and Google, while their silicon approach could provide advantages in terms of operating temperature and manufacturing compatibility. The substantial funding reflects the quantum industry's maturation, as investors increasingly back companies with clear commercialization roadmaps rather than pure research plays.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Quobly, a French quantum computing company, today announced the closing of a €115 million Series A financing to accelerate the industrialization of its silicon-based quantum computers and bring its first commercial product to market by the end of 2026. From technology validation to commercial deployment at scale This Series A […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft Reports Advances in Majorana 2 Following Debate Over Last Year’s Topological Claims
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement represents a crucial moment for Microsoft's controversial topological qubit program, which faced significant scrutiny last year when researchers questioned claims about achieving Majorana particles. The reported 1,000-fold improvement in qubit stability, if validated, could vindicate Microsoft's unique approach and potentially leapfrog current noisy quantum systems that struggle with error rates. Topological qubits promise inherent error protection through their mathematical properties, making this development particularly significant for the field's ultimate goal of fault-tolerant quantum computing. While Microsoft's 2029 timeline for scalable quantum computers remains ambitious, these stability gains suggest their unconventional bet on topological protection may finally be paying dividends.

Insider Brief Microsoft is reporting that it has achieved a more than 1,000-fold improvement in the stability of its topological qubits, a result the company argues brings its long-debated quantum computing approach significantly closer to practical machines and supports a roadmap targeting a scalable quantum computer by 2029. The announcement, detailed in a Microsoft technical […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM and Real Asset Acquisition Corp. Announce Upsized $146 Million PIPE with New Commitment from Ilmarinen
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals growing institutional confidence in quantum hardware companies as pension fund Ilmarinen joins IQM's expanded $146 million PIPE financing alongside their SPAC merger. The upsized funding round demonstrates that major institutional investors are increasingly willing to back quantum computing companies through the challenging path from R&D to commercialization, particularly those like IQM that focus on full-stack superconducting quantum systems. While SPAC deals in quantum have had mixed results, the additional commitment from a major Finnish pension fund suggests sophisticated investors see real value in IQM's European quantum computing approach and customer traction. This funding boost should help IQM accelerate their hardware development and compete more effectively with established players like IBM and emerging rivals in the increasingly crowded quantum hardware space.

Insider Brief IQM Finland Oy, a global leader in full-stack superconducting quantum computers (“IQM”, “IQM Quantum Computers” or the “Company”) and Real Asset Acquisition Corp. (Nasdaq: RAAQ), a special purpose acquisition company (“RAAQ”), today announced an additional PIPE commitment from Ilmarinen in connection with the previously announced business combination between IQM and RAAQ. Ilmarinen’s new commitment comes […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Xanadu Launches Public Cloud Access for Borealis
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone as Xanadu becomes the first company to offer public cloud access to a quantum computer that has demonstrated quantum computational advantage, breaking the traditional barrier between research achievements and practical accessibility. The Borealis system's photonic architecture represents a compelling alternative to the superconducting and trapped-ion approaches that dominate current quantum cloud offerings, potentially offering advantages in operating temperature and network connectivity for future quantum internet applications. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the full programmability of all gates, giving researchers unprecedented flexibility to explore quantum algorithms on a platform that has already proven its computational superiority over classical systems. This democratization of quantum advantage hardware could accelerate algorithm development and bring us closer to discovering the first commercially viable quantum applications.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Xanadu has demonstrated quantum computational advantage using Borealis, their newest photonic quantum computer. It is the first photonic quantum computer offering full programmability of all its gates to demonstrate quantum computational advantage, and the first time that a machine capable of quantum advantage has been made available to the public […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Finland Launches QScale Project to Advance Quantum Computing Scalability
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Finnish initiative represents a crucial step toward solving one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges - scaling up while maintaining energy efficiency and signal precision. The QScale project's focus on optical control and ultra-precise signal technology addresses the fundamental bottleneck that has limited quantum computers to relatively small qubit counts, as traditional electronic control systems become increasingly complex and error-prone at scale. With Finland's strong track record in quantum research and VTT's expertise in photonics, this collaboration could yield breakthrough approaches that make fault-tolerant quantum computers with thousands of qubits more feasible. The project's emphasis on energy efficiency is particularly timely, as the quantum computing field grapples with the environmental impact of scaling up these inherently power-hungry systems.
