Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The QScale joint project coordinated by VTT with Tampere and Aalto Universities has received funding from Business Finland’s prestigious Rise to Challenge Programme. In the project, researchers are developing a radical improvement in the energy efficiency and scalability of quantum computers through optical control and ultra-precise signal technology based on […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantinuum Expands IPO as Valuation Climbs Above $14 Billion
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a potential watershed moment for the quantum computing industry, as Quantinuum's IPO could become the largest public market debut in the sector's history with a valuation exceeding $14 billion. The strong investor demand that prompted the company to expand its share offering from the original plan signals growing mainstream confidence in quantum computing's commercial viability, particularly significant given current market conditions. Quantinuum's public listing would provide crucial visibility into the financial realities of quantum hardware companies and could pave the way for other quantum firms to access public markets. The timing is notable as it comes amid increased competition in quantum computing hardware and growing enterprise interest in quantum cloud services.

Insider Brief Quantinuum is increasing the size of its initial public offering as investor demand continues to build ahead of what could become the largest public-market debut in the history of the quantum computing sector. According to Barron’s, Quantinuum now plans to offer 26.5 million shares priced between $53 and $55 each, up from an […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: The Conversation: Quantum Computers Could Expose Our Digital Secrets – But there are Much Better Reasons to Build them
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of the most persistent misconceptions about quantum computing - that breaking encryption is the primary or only reason to develop these machines. While quantum computers will indeed pose challenges to current cryptographic systems, this piece from Royal Holloway researchers emphasizes the far more exciting potential applications, from drug discovery and materials science to optimization problems that could revolutionize logistics and finance. The timing is particularly relevant as we're seeing increased investment in quantum-safe cryptography alongside rapid progress in quantum hardware, making it crucial for the public to understand that quantum computing's true promise extends far beyond cybersecurity concerns. This balanced perspective helps counter the often sensationalized "quantum apocalypse" narrative while highlighting why quantum computing represents one of the most transformative technologies of our time.

By Keith Martin, Professor, Information Security Group, Royal Holloway, University of London and Briana Bowen, Postgraduate research student, Department of Information Security, Royal Holloway, University of London for The Conversation Quantum computers are coming. Or, at least, that’s what current predictions say. These machines harness the power of quantum mechanics, the set of rules governing […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: At Its First NYSE Investor Day, D-Wave Bets on Two Quantum Machines, Not One
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights D-Wave's strategic pivot toward a dual-technology approach, combining their established quantum annealing systems with gate-based quantum computers to address different problem types. D-Wave's CEO taking the unusual step of encouraging investor skepticism demonstrates remarkable transparency in an industry often criticized for overhyped promises, potentially setting a new standard for how quantum companies communicate with stakeholders. The timing of this investor day at the NYSE signals D-Wave's confidence in their commercial viability as they position themselves to compete across multiple quantum computing paradigms. This dual-machine strategy could prove crucial as the quantum industry matures and different applications require specialized quantum architectures.

Insider Brief On June 1, D-Wave Quantum held its first investor day at the New York Stock Exchange under the banner “The D-Wave Difference.” Alan Baratz, CEO of D-Wave, opened by urging the room to be skeptical of quantum computing companies, his own included. Quantum mechanics is hard, the chief executive said, and that makes […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Amazon Braket launches Rigetti Cepheus™-1-108Q superconducting device
item.description:
Curator's Take: This announcement marks a significant milestone in making high-quality quantum hardware more accessible, as Amazon Braket now offers Rigetti's latest 108-qubit superconducting processor to cloud users worldwide. The Cepheus-1-108Q represents a substantial leap in qubit count for Rigetti's publicly available systems, potentially enabling researchers to explore quantum algorithms and applications that require larger, more complex quantum circuits than previously possible through cloud platforms. This expansion of Braket's hardware portfolio is particularly noteworthy because it democratizes access to cutting-edge quantum processors, allowing smaller research groups and companies to experiment with near-term quantum computing without the massive infrastructure investments typically required. The availability of diverse quantum hardware architectures through a single cloud interface continues to accelerate the pace of quantum algorithm development and benchmarking across the field.

This post was contributed to by Zia Mohammad, Scott Smart, Yuvraj Mohan, Mike Piech, and Rebecca Malamud Figure 1: A Rigetti quantum processing unit (QPU) inside a dilution refrigerator. Photo by Drew Bird. Amazon Braket enables customers to design and run quantum algorithms on a broad selection of quantum hardware through a unified interface. On […]

Read original at AWS Quantum Computing Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Matter may entangle with light far more easily near quantum critical points
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's biggest challenges: scaling entanglement from tiny laboratory systems to large-scale materials that could enable practical quantum technologies. The discovery that quantum critical points - special phase transitions where materials hover between different quantum states - dramatically enhance matter-light entanglement could unlock new pathways for quantum communication and sensing applications. By identifying conditions where macroscopic quantum effects naturally emerge, Si's work bridges the gap between fundamental quantum physics and engineering quantum devices that work at room temperature and larger scales. This finding could accelerate development of quantum networks that rely on strong matter-light interactions, potentially making quantum internet infrastructure more feasible.

Quantum entanglement is a state in which particles are entwined with each other. In this entwined state, the properties of one particle influence the other, even when they aren't physically close to each other. This phenomenon has often been observed in small quantum systems with only a few particles in them, where researchers can use it to store and process quantum information. Rice University professor Qimiao Si is interested in understanding and applying quantum entanglement to macroscopic systems with vast numbers of particles.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Simulation of Nucleon-Antinucleon Interaction in Large-$N$ QCD$_2$ on an IBM Quantum Nighthawk Processor
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article demonstrates a fascinating bridge between quantum field theory and practical quantum computing by simulating the fundamental interaction between matter and antimatter particles using IBM's quantum hardware. The researchers cleverly mapped a complex problem in quantum chromodynamics - specifically how nucleons and antinucleons interact - onto a spin chain model that could be implemented on current noisy quantum devices. What makes this particularly impressive is their use of structured error cancellation techniques that allowed them to extract meaningful physics results despite the inherent noise in today's quantum processors. This work showcases how quantum computers are beginning to tackle genuine problems in high-energy physics that are computationally intractable for classical computers, potentially opening new avenues for understanding the fundamental forces of nature.

We report a quantum simulation of the nucleon--antinucleon interaction in large-$N$ two-dimensional quantum chromodynamics (QCD$_2$) on the IBM Quantum Nighthawk processor. In the large-$N$ limit, QCD$_2$ admits a bosonized description in which baryons emerge as topological solitons (kinks) of an effective mesonic field theory, providing a controlled, nonperturbative framework for baryon--antibaryon dynamics. We formulate the problem by mapping the continuum bosonized Hamiltonian to a spin-chain representation equivalent to an XXZ model with anisotropy set by the QCD parameters. In this mapping, nucleon and antinucleon states correspond to kink and antikink excitations, respectively, while their interaction is encoded in the spin correlations of the chain. Using Jordan--Wigner encoding, we implement the resulting XXZ Hamiltonian on a finite set of qubits and realize it via a variational ground state ansatz and postselected nonunitary disorder operator insertions optimized for the Nighthawk architecture. We then show the kink--antikink interaction potential built from the conditional energies of these nonunitary string operators can be robustly extracted from the quantum hardware due to structured error cancelation. The resulting potential exhibits the expected attractive behavior. The quantum simulation results are benchmarked against exact diagonalization, ideal statevector evaluation showing good agreement. To connect the device result to the continuum field theory, we extract the potential in the continuum limit using large-$L$ matrix product state calculations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly
